title: Asset Reconstruction Companies (ARC) and SARFAESI Act
focus: Mechanisms for NPA resolution, powers under SARFAESI, and borrower rights via DRT.
rationale: SARFAESI and DRT are foundational concepts in banking regulation and NPA recovery, frequently tested in UPSC.
sectionExcerpt: ARC is a company incorporated under the Companies Act and registered with RBI under SARFAESI Act. Court-Free Enforcement: Secured creditors like banks and financial institutions, can take possession and sell assets except for agricultural land under Section 13 without judicial approval. Central Database: Established Central Registry for registration of transaction of securitisation and reconstruction of financial assets. Borrower Rights: Borrowers can approach the Debt Recovery Tribunal (DRT) to rectify their grievances against the creditor or authorised officer. DRT are established under the Recovery of Debts and Bankruptcy Act, 1993.
evidenceBullets: ARCs are registered with RBI under the SARFAESI Act.
SARFAESI allows secured creditors to take possession and sell assets without judicial approval, except for agricultural land.
Borrowers can approach the Debt Recovery Tribunal (DRT) for grievances.
DRTs are established under the Recovery of Debts and Bankruptcy Act, 1993.

title: SWIFT Messaging Network and ISO 20022
focus: Global financial messaging standards and alternatives like BRICS Pay.
rationale: International payment systems and alternatives like BRICS Pay are highly relevant for UPSC Economy and International Relations.
sectionExcerpt: Indian banks have accelerated adoption of the SWIFT messaging framework, ISO 20022. The ISO 20022 is an open global standard for financial information. About SWIFT (Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication) It is a secure messaging network facilitating global financial transactions between over 11,000 institutions across 200 countries. Genesis: Founded in 1973 by 239 banks from 15 countries. Headquarters: Belgium. Note: BRICS Pay is a proposed cross-border payment system to facilitate trade and financial transactions among BRICS member to reduce dependence on SWIFT.
evidenceBullets: ISO 20022 is an open global standard for financial information.
SWIFT is a secure messaging network for global financial transactions, headquartered in Belgium.
BRICS Pay is a proposed cross-border payment system to reduce dependence on SWIFT.

title: India Post Payments Bank (IPPB) and Payment Banks
focus: Structure, functions, and regulatory limits of Payment Banks in India.
rationale: Payment banks have specific regulatory constraints such as deposit limits and no lending, which are classic UPSC prelims traps.
sectionExcerpt: Genesis: 2018 as a 100% government-owned payments bank under the Department of Posts, Ministry of Communications. Payment banks are specialized financial institutions designed to provide basic banking services to the underbanked and unbanked population. Aim: Leverages the extensive postal network to bring banking services to the doorstep of every citizen. Services offered: Saving/ Current account, Direct Benefits Transfers, Bill and Utility Payments, access to 3rd Party products such as loans, insurance, etc. It can accept deposits (up to 2 lakh), and enable money transfers and bill payments, but cannot give loans or issue credit cards.
evidenceBullets: IPPB is a 100% government-owned payments bank under the Department of Posts.
Payment banks can accept deposits up to 2 lakh.
Payment banks cannot give loans or issue credit cards.
They offer services like Direct Benefits Transfers, bill payments, and access to 3rd party products like insurance.

title: Prompt Corrective Action (PCA) Framework and Capital Adequacy Ratio
focus: RBI's supervisory action framework for banks and NBFCs, and the definition of CAR.
rationale: PCA and CAR are critical banking regulation tools used by RBI to maintain financial stability.
sectionExcerpt: About 500 Primary Urban Co-operative Banks (UCBs) under the RBI's supervisory action framework will shift to the Prompt Corrective Action (PCA) framework from April 1, 2025. RBI had introduced a PCA Framework for Scheduled Commercial Banks in 2002 & for UCB in July, 2024. PCA framework for UCBs replaced the existing Supervisory Action Framework (SAF). Applicability: It applies to both banks and NBFCs. Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR), Asset Quality and Profitability are the key areas for monitoring. CAR is the ratio of total capital to total risk-weighted assets in percentage terms. It is also called Capital to Risk-weighted Asset Ratio (CRAR).
evidenceBullets: PCA framework applies to both banks and NBFCs.
For UCBs, the PCA framework replaced the Supervisory Action Framework (SAF).
Key monitoring areas under PCA include Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR), Asset Quality, and Profitability.
CAR (or CRAR) is the ratio of total capital to total risk-weighted assets in percentage terms.

title: Financial Fraud Risk Indicator (FRI)
focus: Risk-based metric for mobile numbers associated with financial fraud.
rationale: Emerging cybersecurity and financial fraud initiatives are relevant for both Economy and Internal Security.
sectionExcerpt: Launched in May 2025 by DoT's Digital Intelligence Unit (DIU). A risk-based metric that classifies a mobile number to have been associated with Medium, High, or Very High risk of financial fraud. This classification is an outcome of inputs obtained from various stakeholders including reporting on Indian Cyber Crime Coordination Centre (I4C's) National Cybercrime Reporting Portal (NCRP), DoT's Chakshu platform, and Intelligence shared by banks and financial institutions.
evidenceBullets: FRI is launched by the Department of Telecommunications' (DoT) Digital Intelligence Unit (DIU).
It classifies mobile numbers into Medium, High, or Very High risk of financial fraud.
Inputs are gathered from I4C's NCRP, DoT's Chakshu platform, and banks.
